title: Jeremiah
author: Jeremiah son of Hilkiah, with his secretary Baruch son of Neriah
date: ~627–580 BC (Jeremiah's ministry; the book was compiled over this period)
setting: Judah's final decades before the Babylonian exile — from Josiah's reign through the fall of Jerusalem (586 BC) and the flight to Egypt, where Jeremiah dies.
purpose: To call Judah to repentance in the face of certain judgment, to announce the new covenant (ch. 31) that would replace the broken Sinai covenant, and to show that the prophet who speaks God's word faithfully will suffer rejection.
themes: The new covenant written on the heart (31:31-34)
Faithless covenant — Judah as an unfaithful wife
Jeremiah's confessions — the cost of prophetic ministry
Babylon as God's instrument of judgment
The Rechabites as a model of covenant faithfulness
Restoration after exile (chs. 30–33, 'the Book of Comfort')

outline.label: Temple Sermons and Conflict with False Prophets
The Book of Comfort: New Covenant
The Fall of Jerusalem and Its Aftermath

before.label: Isaiah — judgment and redemption announced a century earlier
Josiah's reform — the last window for national repentance
Josiah dies at Megiddo; reform unravels

after.label: Fall of Jerusalem; temple destroyed by Nebuchadnezzar
New Covenant fulfilled in Christ's Last Supper

key_people.name: Jeremiah ben Hilkiah
key_people.role: Prophet from Anathoth, called before birth (1:5) to preach a message of imminent judgment for over 40 years; his deep personal suffering, preserved in his Confessions (11–20), makes him the OT's most transparently human prophet

key_people.name: Baruch ben Neriah
key_people.role: Jeremiah's secretary who wrote the prophecies at dictation (36:4), preserved them through the destruction of the first scroll, and accompanied Jeremiah to Egypt — a crucial figure in the book's transmission

key_people.name: Josiah
key_people.role: The reforming king whose death at Megiddo (609 BC) marks the end of Judah's last opportunity for genuine repentance and sets the stage for the irreversible slide toward judgment

key_people.name: Zedekiah
key_people.role: The last king of Judah, who is neither able to believe Jeremiah's word nor able to defy his nobles; his personal conversations with Jeremiah are the book's most psychologically complex scenes

key_people.name: Nebuchadnezzar
key_people.role: The Babylonian king whom God calls 'my servant' (25:9) — the instrument of divine judgment, whose conquest of Jerusalem fulfils every warning Jeremiah had given over four decades

context: Jeremiah ministered from 627 BC (Josiah's 13th year) to after Jerusalem's fall in 587 BC and possibly into the Egyptian exile. He witnessed Josiah's reformation, its collapse, and the progressive Babylonian invasions of 605, 597, and 587 BC. His message was uniquely difficult: not 'trust God and be saved' but 'God's judgment is coming — submit to Babylon as his instrument.' This made him appear a traitor and cost him his freedom, his standing, and very nearly his life.
christ_connection: Jeremiah's 'new covenant' prophecy (31:31–34) is the most explicit OT promise of what the gospel accomplishes: a law written on the heart rather than tablets of stone, a direct and universal knowledge of God, and a complete and non-repeatable forgiveness of sin. Jesus explicitly fulfils this prophecy at the Last Supper ('this cup is the new covenant in my blood', Luke 22:20; 1 Cor 11:25), and Hebrews 8–10 develops the argument at length. Jeremiah himself, in his suffering for faithfully proclaiming God's word despite rejection, prefigures the Suffering Servant and the rejected prophet who is the Christ.

key_verses.note: 'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you... I appointed you a prophet to the nations' — God's sovereign call that sustains Jeremiah through decades of rejection and suffering
'The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick' — the diagnosis of human nature that explains why external religious reform without inner transformation always fails
'For I know the plans I have for you... plans for welfare and not for evil' — a promise to exiles in Babylon, not a general prosperity gospel, that God's purposes survive even catastrophic judgment
The new covenant promise — God will write his law on hearts, forgive completely, and make knowledge of himself universal — the most programmatic OT anticipation of the gospel

themes_detail.title: The Failure of Covenant Faithfulness and the Certainty of Judgment
themes_detail.text: Jeremiah's central message is that Judah has broken the Mosaic covenant so thoroughly and so persistently that the covenant curses of Deuteronomy (exile, destruction of city and temple) are now inevitable. The Temple Sermon (ch. 7) is the book's theological manifesto: the temple is not a magic talisman that protects Jerusalem regardless of the people's conduct. Repentance is possible (3:12–14; 18:7–10) but in practice is not forthcoming, and by the time of Zedekiah's reign the window has closed.

themes_detail.title: The Confessions and the Cost of Prophetic Ministry
themes_detail.text: Jeremiah's Confessions (11:18–23; 12:1–6; 15:10–21; 17:14–18; 18:19–23; 20:7–18) are unique in prophetic literature: they record his raw protest to God about the cost of his calling, his accusation that God has 'deceived' him (20:7), and his cursing the day of his birth. These are not editorial failures but theological treasures: they demonstrate that faithful service to God does not insulate from suffering, that lament is a legitimate response to vocation, and that God can be argued with. God's responses to the Confessions never dissolve the suffering but consistently recommit Jeremiah to his task.

themes_detail.title: The New Covenant as the Answer to the Old Covenant's Failure
themes_detail.text: The book of consolation (chs. 30–33) stands as a light in the darkness of Jeremiah's relentlessly gloomy message. The new covenant promise (31:31–34) does not simply reiterate the old covenant's demands but announces a structural transformation: the law will be internalized, knowledge of God will be universal and direct, and forgiveness will be complete and non-repeatable. This is not merely a better version of Sinai but a different kind of relationship — one that the Holy Spirit will accomplish in Christ's people. Hebrews argues that this very promise proves the old covenant's inadequacy and Christ's superiority.

themes_detail.title: Surrender to God's Purposes Even in Catastrophe
themes_detail.text: Jeremiah's theology of submission — buying a field at Anathoth just as the Babylonians arrive (ch. 32), writing to the exiles to settle in Babylon and seek its welfare (ch. 29), urging Zedekiah to submit to Nebuchadnezzar — is not defeatism but a form of eschatological faith. God is sovereign over Babylon; his purposes will be accomplished through, not despite, the catastrophe; the future belongs to those who receive his judgment honestly and trust his long-term intentions. This posture is the OT form of the NT call to take up one's cross.
